title: Quick check: clean uninstall and decommission

choices: An organisation-specific credential exposed under the customer's organisation Settings in the Huntress portal; authorises the agent to uninstall cleanly. Per-organisation, not per-endpoint.

choices.label: A one-time-use code generated per endpoint.
choices.rationale: The key is per-org, not per-endpoint.

choices.label: An installer command-line argument that auto-disables the agent.
choices.rationale: Conflates the key with the install path; the key authorises the uninstall.

choices.label: A senior-only credential the helpdesk can't see.
choices.rationale: The helpdesk uses it via the runbook in their scope.

questions.explanation: Per-organisation credential, lives in the portal's organisation Settings.

questions.prompt: Why is the local uninstall gated by the key?
choices: To prevent attackers from removing the agent silently.

choices.label: To make uninstalls slower for billing purposes.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Because the agent has no uninstall code without the key.
choices.rationale: The code is there; the gate authorises it.

choices.label: To make the SOC notice removals.
choices.rationale: Not how the SOC operates.

questions.explanation: The gate is intentional security design.

questions.prompt: 'Skipping the uninstall key and running the installer's uninstall mode without it' — what's the consequence?
choices: The gated uninstall either fails or removes the binary without cleanly deregistering. The portal then shows a stale online agent that has actually gone away, or a partial-uninstall state that confuses the next tech.

choices.label: Nothing; the uninstall succeeds cleanly without the key.
choices.rationale: Inverts the design.

choices.label: The endpoint reboots automatically.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

choices.label: Huntress charges a 'forced uninstall' fee.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

questions.explanation: Both ends of the uninstall (endpoint and portal) need to land cleanly; the key is what makes both happen.

questions.prompt: Why is 'pasting the uninstall key into chat or a customer email' called out as wrong?
choices: Same family as the install-time org-key mistake. The key is a credential; treat it as one.

choices.label: Chat platforms are insecure but email is fine.
choices.rationale: Both surfaces are wrong.

choices.label: Customer emails get audited by Huntress.
choices.rationale: Not how the platform works.

choices.label: It's a copyright violation.
choices.rationale: Wildly out of scope.

questions.explanation: Credential discipline applies; use the runbook's documented surface.

questions.prompt: Order the steps for a single-endpoint clean uninstall.
items: Confirm the endpoint and the reason from the PSA ticket or request.
Pull the organisation-specific uninstall key from the portal Settings (or from the runbook's vault if pre-staged).
Run the uninstall via RMM (or the documented manual command for resistant agents) using the org's uninstall key.
Wait for the agent to process the uninstall.
Verify in the Huntress portal that the agent has moved to offline and then disappeared (or moved to the expected post-uninstall state).
Verify on the endpoint if possible: Huntress service gone, install path empty.
Document in the PSA: endpoint name, uninstall timestamp, reason, any complication.
questions.explanation: Confirm reason, pull key, run uninstall, wait, verify portal, verify endpoint, document.

questions.prompt: A leaving employee's laptop was wiped to factory state yesterday. The customer's IT manager asks you to 'remove Huntress so we can re-deploy from scratch.' What's the right interpretation?

choices.label: Run the uninstall via RMM with the organisation's uninstall key.
choices.rationale: Whether the agent still exists on the wiped laptop depends on what the wipe touched. The portal will tell you in 30 seconds.

choices: Check the portal first. If the agent is still registered, run the documented uninstall with the org key. If the agent isn't registered, the wipe already removed it; the laptop will need a fresh install when redeployed.

choices.label: Skip the uninstall; the laptop was wiped, the agent is gone.
choices.rationale: Wipes vary; some leave services in place, some don't.

questions.explanation: Two minutes of checking saves a redundant uninstall attempt and tells you what the next step actually is.

questions.prompt: An agent shows offline since yesterday. The customer's IT manager asks: 'Can you just remove it from the portal so we have a clean list?'

choices.label: Click the 'remove from portal' / 'delete agent' option in the Agents view.
choices.rationale: Some MSPs treat 'delete from portal' as a senior-only destructive admin action.

choices: Confirm the runbook's policy on removing decommissioned agents from the portal view. If it's in your scope, do it; if not, bump.

choices.label: Ignore the request; the agent is offline, the customer is happy.
choices.rationale: The customer asked for a clean view; the action is small but whether you can do it is what's worth checking.

questions.explanation: The runbook decides whether this is helpdesk scope or senior scope.

questions.prompt: The agent resists the uninstall even with the documented manual command. What's the guidance?
choices: Investigate the cause (tamper-protection, permissions, AV interference) before forcing a retry. Bump if you can't identify the cause.

choices.label: Force a retry every 10 minutes until it takes.
choices.rationale: Brute-force without cause-investigation.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket and stop work.
choices.rationale: Premature; investigate locally first via the senior route.

choices.label: Re-install the agent and try again later.
choices.rationale: Adds complexity without addressing the resistance.

questions.explanation: Cause-investigation before brute-force; senior bump if cause isn't findable.

questions.prompt: 'Leaving the endpoint in the portal as offline forever because nobody clicked through the uninstall' — what's the issue?
choices: The agent is still installed, still trying to phone home, still occupying a license slot. The decommission isn't real until the agent is off.

choices.label: Abandoned offline agents auto-delete after 30 days.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

choices.label: Abandoned offlines are billed at half-rate.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: The SOC closes incidents on offline endpoints automatically.
choices.rationale: Not how the SOC operates.

questions.explanation: Offline in the portal isn't decommissioned; the agent is still on the endpoint.

questions.prompt: The endpoint's owner asks to keep Huntress on it after decommission 'for the next user.' What's the guidance?

choices.label: Just leave it on; one less re-deploy.
choices.rationale: Bypasses the senior's lifecycle conversation with the customer.

choices.label: Uninstall now and re-install when the next user gets the endpoint.
choices.rationale: The right action depends on the lifecycle decision; the tech doesn't make it.

choices.label: Ask the customer's IT contact to decide.
choices.rationale: The customer-side conversation is the senior's surface.

questions.explanation: Coverage decisions across asset lifecycles aren't tech-level calls.
